## [03:00 - 06:02]

And this is going to feed back into the whole concept of think different because Mulke also had another interesting quote where he said that all encounters with the enemy, I'm paraphrasing again, excuse me, what he said was that all generals see the battlefield as a system of options. So, in other words, that first encounter, you know, your initial battle plan that you plan for, maybe, you know, days or weeks in advance, it doesn't survive that first encounter. So, the good generals are the ones who see that battlefield and then they say, okay, I can see how this is playing out, this is where I need to move the pieces correctly. So, just like those great generals, the best tennis players, they've done that SWAT analysis, and then once the match gets going, they come back to D&O, they're like, okay, I've got this big toolbox of tactics I can use based on the evolution of the match. So, if, you know, plan A is not working, tactic A, if that is not working, I can go to tactic B, I can go to tactic C, because again, the tennis match, it's a series of options, it's a series of different tactics you can draw on to obviously, you know, turn things in your favor. Well, Federer was going to play aggressive first strike tennis. It's what he was doing at the end of last year under the tutelage of Paul Anacone. It's what he did over the course of the earlier rounds of the Aussie Open. He was having great success with just playing very aggressive tennis and very penetrating tennis, moving forward into the court. Now, Djokovic, he obviously knew that, he knew how Federer had been playing. So, what Djokovic wanted to do was play very solid defense. He wanted to hold Federer back. He wanted to force Federer to hit a series of big shots from behind the baseline to win a point. And his hope there, his goal was that Federer wouldn't be able to string together a series of big shots. He would start making too many errors and that would allow Djokovic to win that match. So, what happened in the first set and then early in the second set is, first of all, it was a tight match, but Federer wasn't quite able to string together enough big shots to win the first. And then in the second, he actually got down a break. And Djokovic was playing great defense. He was able to do exactly what he wanted to do, which was to hold Federer behind the baseline. Because what Fed likes to do with that penetrating style of play, he's really looking for a ball in the middle of the court here, preferably on the forehand side. If he gets that, and he's inside the baseline, the point's pretty much over. Quick reminder, by the way, the circle

## [06:02 - 09:06]

has the player stick as the side. They're hitting on, forehand for righty. This is a backhand for righty, and it's reversed up there. This is a backhand for Djokovic, forehand for Djokovic. So Federer knew this, and Federer was saying to himself, okay, my initial battle plan didn't survive that first encounter. So what adjustment can I make to specifically get some balls in this area of the court where I can continue to move forward? And what's really important to understand is, if your initial plan, your initial battle plan isn't working, that doesn't mean you just completely take it and throw it out the window. So from the tennis perspective, if you're a counterpuncher, for example, and your initial plan isn't working too well, that doesn't mean you suddenly start to, you know, to become a serve and volleyer. That's not going to work. But it does make you make it, it does mean you make adjustments here and there and play within the framework of your overall game and what you're good at. That's what these series of options really means in terms of the tennis match. So the adjustment Federer made was during the baseline rally when nobody had control, Federer started throwing in more slices and some moon balls as well. So in other words, he started throwing a lot of variety at Djokovic because previously, like we said, Federer was just trying to hit a bigger ball. And that ball was a little bit more standardized, a lot of pace, but it allowed Djokovic to get grooved. So Djokovic was able to play effective defense. But when Federer started throwing in some slice, a little bit more air, and then of course, the aggressively hit balls as well, Djokovic had, you know, his timing went off a little bit. So not only did he make some errors, just gave Fed some free points, he also left some balls short. So then Federer was able to step in, he was able to penetrate the court more, which had been his overarching strategy coming into the match. And again, once he's in here, then he's really, you know, he's in control of the point, despite how good, you know, despite Djokovic's defense, which is phenomenal, you give Federer a ball inside the baseline, he's going to win that point most of the time. So that adjustment worked. And like I said, before Federer was down a break early in the second, he actually ended up going up 5-2 in the second set. But then for whatever reason, Federer decided to kind of go back to his old strategy, which is just sort of hit big off the ground. He kind of got away from that variety. Then Djokovic won the second set, came back, and then he won the third set, obviously winning in straight sets. Now, what's really, you know, important to highlight here is that a tennis match is a troubleshooting exercise. It's not like these guys come into the match and they say, okay, if plan, you know, if my opponent does this, I'm going to do this, but if my opponent does this, I'm going to do this, and so on. They're making adjustments on the fly. They're looking at the battlefield, and they're saying, okay, this is what's going on. I've evaluated the situation correctly. Now,

## [09:06 - 12:10]

this is what I need to do. So it's not like, again, for clarity's sake, they know exactly what's going to happen before the match even starts. And Djokovic had a telling quote in terms of this notion when he was interviewed after the match. They said, what would you have done if you had lost the second set? And he said, I don't know. I don't, you know, the dynamics of the match would have been totally different. Well, what Djokovic would have done is he would have on the changeover in between sets thought to himself, what did Federer do to win that second set? And what do I need to do to counter his adjustment? So the way this works at the pro level and the way we want it to work, the way you want it to work for you is you have that initial battle plan, the player who starts losing is going to make an adjustment, then you adjust to his adjustment, then he's going to adjust to your adjustment and so on. It's literally a chess match like that, where guys are going back and forth evaluating what the other person is trying to do to them. And like I said earlier, most rec players, they only focus on themselves. What am I good at? What am I not good at? They never think about what's going on on the other end of the court. What their opponent is doing to them to exploit what, you know, the weaknesses and try and keep the ball away from the strength. So they never think about how is my opponent trying to hurt me. hitting some winners, or forcing you to go back and reset the point.
